channel.description: Latest UK property and mortgage news from leading industry blogs.

item.title: What London’s 90-Night Rule Means for Serviced Flat Investors
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

For property investors focusing on serviced accommodation in London, understanding the 90-night rule is crucial. This regulation, which limits the number of nights a property can be let out for short-term stays, carries significant implications. Breaching it could lead to enforcement action, hefty fines, or even a planning injunction halting operations. Here’s a closer look at what the rule entails, who it applies to, and how investors can structure their portfolios to stay compliant.

What is the 90-Night Rule?

The 90-night rule restricts the use of residential properties in London for short-term lets to a maximum of 90 nights per calendar year. Introduced by the Greater London Authority, this regulation aims to balance the growing demand for short-term rentals with the need to preserve housing availability for long-term residents. Properties used for short-term lets beyond this limit must obtain planning permission for change of use to a “temporary sleeping accommodation” category.

This rule applies to platforms like Airbnb, Booking.com, and other short-term rental services. It’s particularly relevant for investors in serviced apartments, as non-compliance can result in severe consequences, including fines of up to £20,000 per offence.

Who is Exempt from the Rule?

Certain types of accommodation are exempt from the 90-night rule. These include hotels, hostels, and properties that already have planning permission for short-term lets. Additionally, landlords who rent out their primary residence for fewer than 90 nights a year are also unaffected. However, investors operating serviced flats as a business must adhere strictly to the regulation unless they secure the necessary permissions.

It’s worth noting that some boroughs may have additional restrictions or requirements, so investors should always check local planning policies before proceeding.

Structuring Investments for Compliance

For serviced accommodation investors, staying within the 90-night limit requires careful planning. One approach is to diversify portfolios by including properties outside London or focusing on long-term rentals. Alternatively, investors might consider applying for planning permission to legally exceed the limit, though this process can be time-consuming and costly.

Another strategy is to partner with professional property management companies that specialise in navigating these regulations. These firms can help ensure compliance while maximising rental yields. Additionally, using technology to track booking nights and manage occupancy rates can prevent accidental breaches of the rule.

Navigating London’s 90-night rule demands a proactive approach for serviced accommodation investors. By understanding the regulation, identifying exemptions, and structuring investments thoughtfully, landlords can avoid penalties and operate successfully within the legal framework. Always consulting with property experts or legal advisors is advisable to ensure full compliance and protect your investment.

item.title: Landlord Law Newsround #444
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

The government has unveiled further details about its upcoming landlord ombudsman scheme, which will extend the existing Housing Ombudsman Service currently serving social housing tenants. The new system aims to create consistency across both private and social rental sectors, with binding compensation awards of up to £25,000 available to tenants. While no exact start date has been confirmed, housing minister Baroness Taylor of Stevenage has promised landlords will receive adequate notice before mandatory participation begins.

Heatwave risks bring new responsibilities for landlords

Landlords could face fines of up to £7,000 if their rental properties become dangerously hot during heatwaves. Under the Housing Health and Safety Rating System (HHSRS), overheating that creates health risks may lead to penalties, particularly where ventilation systems are faulty. Whilst there's no legal maximum temperature requirement, broken extractor fans, jammed windows or other ventilation failures that compromise air flow could trigger enforcement action. Property experts emphasise that the key factor isn't a specific temperature threshold, but whether conditions become hazardous enough to affect tenants' wellbeing.

Disability discrimination considerations in eviction cases

With Section 21 'no fault' evictions being phased out, landlords relying on rent arrears or anti-social behaviour claims should be aware of potential disability discrimination counterclaims. Under the Equality Act 2010, tenants may argue that their disability contributed to any payment issues or behavioural problems. Courts must consider such arguments when assessing possession cases, meaning landlords need to carefully document all communications and demonstrate reasonable adjustments have been made where appropriate.

These developments highlight the evolving responsibilities facing landlords in England's private rental sector. Staying informed about regulatory changes and maintaining thorough property records could help landlords navigate these new requirements whilst protecting their interests.

item.title: Property professionals offered free AML webinar
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Property professionals looking to stay up to date with evolving anti-money laundering (AML) requirements may benefit from a new free webinar. Hosted by FCS Compliance, the 30-minute session aims to clarify recent changes to Money Laundering Regulations and their practical impact on estate and letting agents.

Understanding the AML updates

The webinar, scheduled for Thursday 16 July at 1pm, comes shortly after HM Treasury announced amendments to the Money Laundering Regulations on 30 June. With further guidance expected soon, the session could help property businesses prepare for upcoming compliance obligations. Stephen Williamson, Lead AML Consultant at FCS Compliance, will guide attendees through the key changes and their implications.

Topics will include how the updated regulations affect estate agents' daily operations, along with anticipated HMRC expectations regarding risk assessments and customer due diligence. The session will particularly focus on practical adjustments that firms might need to make to their existing AML processes.

Key areas of focus

Property professionals attending the webinar can expect detailed coverage of several critical compliance areas:

  • The specific impact of regulation changes on estate and letting agents
  • How forthcoming HMRC guidance may influence AML compliance practices
  • Required updates to risk assessments, policies and procedures
  • When to review customer due diligence and enhanced due diligence measures
  • Identifying high-risk clients and transaction warning signs
  • Essential record-keeping, training and audit trail requirements

With HMRC increasing its focus on AML compliance in the property sector, the webinar may prove particularly valuable for businesses seeking to strengthen their controls. The session will offer actionable steps to help firms prepare for potential inspections whilst maintaining robust anti-money laundering processes.

Those interested in attending the free webinar can register through FCS Compliance's website. As regulatory requirements continue to evolve, staying informed about AML obligations remains crucial for all property professionals operating in the UK market.

item.title: Rent controls would hurt fewer landlords than tax hikes, report claims
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

A new study suggests that well-designed rent controls could ease financial pressures on tenants with less impact on landlords than recent tax and interest rate changes. Research from University College London and the New Economics Foundation indicates that whilst rent freezes might affect landlord profitability, existing fiscal policies have already created greater challenges for property investors.

Comparing policy impacts on landlords

The report analysed HMRC data to model different rent reduction scenarios. A 10% cut – equivalent to freezing rents at May 2024 levels – would reportedly make just 2.3% of individual landlords unprofitable. By comparison, 4.8% of landlords have already been pushed into the red by interest rate rises and tax reforms introduced since 2021. Even a 20% rent reduction would leave mortgaged landlords with returns above average UK business profits, whilst outright owners would maintain higher yields.

For tenants, the potential savings could be significant. The analysis estimates average households might save £1,300 annually under a 10% reduction, rising to £2,400 with 20% cuts. Such measures could also reduce housing benefit expenditure by at least £2 billion per year in the more ambitious scenario.

Potential opportunities in housing market shifts

Contrary to concerns about mass landlord exodus, the report highlights that England's private rented sector actually grew by approximately 96,000 properties between 2023-2025. Whilst some landlords might sell if rent controls were introduced, researchers suggest this could create acquisition opportunities for social housing providers.

Dr Beth Stratford, the report's lead author, notes: "Landlords continue to achieve strong returns despite recent financial pressures. Well-structured rent controls, alongside complementary policies, could help rebalance the housing market towards more secure and affordable options." The study proposes such measures could facilitate transfers of properties from private rentals into community-led housing schemes or social ownership.

As the debate around housing affordability continues, this research offers fresh perspectives on how policy interventions might affect different stakeholders in the property market. With many households facing rental affordability challenges, the findings may inform ongoing discussions about potential solutions.

item.title: BTL landlords given new support as rules change
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The National Residential Landlords Association (NRLA) has introduced a set of online Knowledge Hubs to assist buy-to-let landlords in managing their properties amidst recent regulatory changes. These hubs aim to provide comprehensive guidance and tools to help landlords comply with the Renters’ Rights Act and streamline their property management processes. The resources cater to landlords at various stages of their investment journey, from newcomers to seasoned property investors.

Navigating Regulatory Changes

The Renters’ Rights Act has introduced new legal responsibilities for landlords, creating a need for accessible and reliable support. The NRLA Knowledge Hubs have been designed to address this, offering tailored content that covers everything from tenancy management to maintenance. The hubs focus on ensuring landlords understand their obligations and can take steps to avoid non-compliance.

One of the key features is the Tenancy Lifecycle Hub, which provides detailed guidance on pre-tenancy checks and other critical stages of managing a rental property. This resource could be particularly valuable for landlords seeking to stay up-to-date with evolving regulations and best practices.

Support for Landlords at Every Stage

The NRLA Knowledge Hubs are structured to accommodate landlords with varying levels of experience. First-time landlords may find the hubs particularly useful, as they offer step-by-step advice on entering the buy-to-let market. Meanwhile, experienced landlords looking to expand their portfolios can access advanced tools and insights to refine their property management strategies.

Ben Beadle, Chief Executive of the NRLA, emphasised the hubs’ role in providing trusted, credible guidance. He highlighted that the hubs are designed as user-friendly portals, offering bespoke solutions to meet the specific needs of landlords across England and Wales. With expert-backed resources, landlords can quickly find answers to even the most niche queries.

Practical Tools for Property Management

In addition to regulatory guidance, the hubs provide practical tools and services to simplify day-to-day property management. These include resources for maintenance planning, tenant communication, and financial management. By consolidating these tools in one place, the NRLA aims to reduce the administrative burden on landlords and help them focus on growing their investments.

The launch of these hubs comes at a time when the private rented sector is undergoing significant changes. For landlords, staying informed and compliant is more important than ever. The NRLA Knowledge Hubs could serve as a valuable resource for those looking to navigate this evolving landscape with confidence.

As the buy-to-let market continues to adapt to new legislation, landlords might consider exploring these hubs to enhance their understanding and management of rental properties. With expert-backed insights and practical tools, the NRLA Knowledge Hubs could prove to be an essential resource for landlords across the UK.

item.title: More housing stock, tougher market – accurate pricing becomes critical
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

The UK property market is showing signs of cooling in 2026, with fewer homes sold subject to contract compared to the previous year. Despite this, transaction levels remain above long-term averages, suggesting a resilient market. With more housing stock available, buyers are benefiting from greater choice, whilst sellers face increased competition, making accurate pricing more crucial than ever.

Market Activity Remains Above Pre-Pandemic Levels

Data from week 26 of 2026 reveals that 23,800 homes were sold subject to contract, slightly down from the previous week’s 24,100. Year-to-date figures show 644,000 homes sold stc, which is 6.8% lower than in 2025. However, this figure still surpasses pre-pandemic levels, with sales 7.3% higher than the 2017–19 average and 10.7% above 2023 levels. This indicates that whilst the market has softened since 2025, it remains robust compared to historical norms.

Net sales, which account for fall-throughs, also reflect this trend. Year-to-date net sales stand at 502,000, 5.2% lower than in 2025 but 5% above the 2017–19 average. Meanwhile, exchanges in June 2026 reached 48,600, with this number expected to rise as more transactions are reported in July.

Increased Housing Stock Puts Pressure on Sellers

Property listings have surged in 2026, with 967,000 new listings year-to-date, matching 2025 levels and exceeding the 2017–19 average by 12.5%. This increase in supply has given buyers more options, intensifying competition among sellers. As a result, price reductions have become more common, with 25,800 properties seeing price cuts in week 26 alone. In June, 14.3% of homes for sale had their prices reduced, up from 13.4% in May.

Withdrawals also rose in June, with 49,000 properties being taken off the market. This figure is expected to climb further as more withdrawals are processed in July. The difference between the asking price of listings and those that go sold stc remains consistent with the long-term average, standing at 16.6% compared to the 10-year average of 16% to 17%.

For sellers, this highlights the importance of setting realistic asking prices to attract buyers and avoid prolonged marketing periods. Accurate pricing could help reduce the likelihood of withdrawals or repeated price reductions.

Whilst the UK property market has seen a slowdown compared to 2025, it continues to perform well against historical benchmarks. With more homes available, buyers are in a stronger position, and sellers may need to strategise carefully to secure a sale in this increasingly competitive environment.

item.title: Interest rates will need to rise, warns Bank of England’s chief economist
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The Bank of England's chief economist has signalled that further interest rate rises may be necessary in the coming months to tackle persistent inflationary pressures. Huw Pill's comments come despite the Monetary Policy Committee (MPC) recently voting to maintain the base rate at 3.75% for the fourth consecutive meeting.

Warning over persistent inflation risks

Speaking to BBC Walescast, Pill expressed concerns that the UK economy has been "running hotter" than its productive capacity could sustain. The chief economist warned this imbalance between demand and supply could prolong inflationary pressures, stating "the short answer is yes" when asked whether rates might need to rise in the next year.

Inflation currently stands at 2.8% - significantly lower than the double-digit peaks seen in 2022 but still above the Bank's 2% target. Pill was one of two MPC members who voted for an immediate rate increase at the last meeting, suggesting growing unease about inflation becoming entrenched in the economy.

Balancing act for policymakers

The MPC faces a challenging task in calibrating monetary policy, needing to balance inflation control against supporting economic growth. Whilst keeping rates unchanged in recent months has provided some stability for borrowers, policymakers remain vigilant about inflation risks.

Homeowners with variable-rate mortgages or those approaching the end of fixed-term deals might consider reviewing their options ahead of the next MPC decision on 30th July. With the potential for further rate rises, borrowers could explore:

  • Fixed-rate mortgage options to lock in current rates
  • Speaking to mortgage brokers about product transfer opportunities
  • Reviewing household budgets to account for potential higher repayments

As the economic landscape continues to evolve, staying informed about monetary policy developments could help homeowners make informed decisions about their mortgage arrangements.

item.title: The mortgage trend that’s gathering pace
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

Recent data highlights a growing preference among UK borrowers for two-year fixed-rate mortgages, as mortgage rates show signs of stabilising and even declining. Analysis from Moneyfactscompare.co.uk reveals that demand for two-year fixed deals has steadily increased over the past few months, whilst interest in five-year fixes has dipped. This trend is particularly noticeable among remortgage borrowers and home movers, suggesting many are anticipating further reductions in mortgage rates.

Why Are Two-Year Fixed Mortgages Gaining Popularity?

The rise in demand for two-year fixed mortgages can be attributed to several factors. Borrowers with larger deposits or higher levels of equity in their homes are finding that two-year fixed rates are increasingly competitive, often slightly cheaper than their five-year equivalents. This pricing dynamic makes shorter-term deals an attractive option for those who may wish to refinance again if rates continue to improve.

Adam French, head of consumer finance at Moneyfactscompare.co.uk, notes, “Many borrowers are opting for the flexibility of a two-year fix, as expectations for lower mortgage rates build. However, this approach isn’t without risk. Recent years have shown that borrowing costs can shift rapidly in volatile economic conditions.”

First-Time Buyers and Higher Loan-to-Value Borrowers Face Different Choices

For first-time buyers and those with smaller deposits, the mortgage landscape differs slightly. At higher loan-to-value (LTV) ratios, five-year fixed mortgages often remain cheaper than two-year deals. This leaves borrowers in this category weighing the benefits of lower initial costs against the flexibility of a shorter fixed term.

French explains, “First-time buyers and borrowers with smaller deposits face a different market. At higher LTVs, five-year fixes frequently offer lower rates than two-year deals, meaning these borrowers must carefully consider their options.”

Data shows that first-time buyers are diversifying their choices rather than overwhelmingly switching to two-year fixes. In contrast, remortgage borrowers and home movers are increasingly leaning towards shorter-term deals, reflecting their confidence in potential rate reductions.

Ultimately, the choice between a two-year and five-year fixed mortgage depends on individual circumstances. Borrowers may wish to consider their financial goals, equity levels, and appetite for risk when making their decision. With mortgage rates continuing to evolve, staying informed and seeking professional advice could help ensure the best outcome.

Borrowers with larger deposits or higher levels of equity in their homes are finding that two-year fixed rates are increasingly competitive, often slightly cheaper than their five-year equivalents. This pricing dynamic makes shorter-term deals an attractive option for those who may wish to refinance again if rates continue to improve.

Adam French, head of consumer finance at Moneyfactscompare.co.uk, notes, “Many borrowers are opting for the flexibility of a two-year fix, as expectations for lower mortgage rates build. However, this approach isn’t without risk. Recent years have shown that borrowing costs can shift rapidly in volatile economic conditions.”

First-Time Buyers and Higher Loan-to-Value Borrowers Face Different Choices

For first-time buyers and those with smaller deposits, the mortgage landscape differs slightly. At higher loan-to-value (LTV) ratios, five-year fixed mortgages often remain cheaper than two-year deals. This leaves borrowers in this category weighing the benefits of lower initial costs against the flexibility of a shorter fixed term.

French explains, “First-time buyers and borrowers with smaller deposits face a different market. At higher LTVs, five-year fixes frequently offer lower rates than two-year deals, meaning these borrowers must carefully consider their options.”

Data shows that first-time buyers are diversifying their choices rather than overwhelmingly switching to two-year fixes. In contrast, remortgage borrowers and home movers are increasingly leaning towards shorter-term deals, reflecting their confidence in potential rate reductions.

Ultimately, the choice between a two-year and five-year fixed mortgage depends on individual circumstances. Borrowers may wish to consider their financial goals, equity levels, and appetite for risk when making their decision. With mortgage rates continuing to evolve, staying informed and seeking professional advice could help ensure the best outcome.

item.title: New build supply hits nine-year low in blow to government’s housing target
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The supply of new build homes in the UK has reached its lowest level in nearly a decade, according to fresh analysis from Rightmove. The findings highlight growing concerns about meeting housing targets, with new development listings falling to their weakest point since early 2017 – despite government ambitions to deliver 1.5 million homes during this parliamentary term.

Market challenges dampen housebuilding

Industry experts point to multiple headwinds affecting new home construction. Elevated mortgage rates continue to create challenging conditions for developers and estate agents alike, whilst affordability pressures persist across many regions. Although overall property listings have risen significantly since 2020, Rightmove notes persistent shortages of appropriately priced homes in desirable locations.

The analysis comes as housing professionals call for government intervention ahead of the Autumn Budget. Suggested measures include stamp duty relief for first-time buyers, enhanced investment in affordable housing schemes, and targeted support to help aspiring homeowners. Such interventions could potentially stimulate market activity whilst addressing critical supply issues.

Calls for renewed first-time buyer support

Industry voices emphasise the pivotal role of first-time buyers in maintaining market momentum. Barratt Redrow's Steve Mariner acknowledges planning system improvements but highlights how regulatory costs and economic uncertainty continue to constrain development. He advocates for a fresh support scheme involving both government and developer contributions to revive first-time buyer activity.

Rightmove's Alex Slater echoes these concerns, noting the growing challenges facing prospective homeowners. "The absence of meaningful support schemes in England comes at precisely the wrong time," Slater observes. The property expert calls for coordinated reforms spanning taxation policies and affordable housing investment to improve accessibility for new entrants.

As the housing market navigates these complex conditions, industry stakeholders await potential policy responses that could help bridge the gap between housing ambitions and delivery realities. The coming months may prove crucial for determining whether supply can rebound to meet both political targets and buyer demand.

item.title: Historic estate agency acquired by regional property group
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Castles Property Group has announced the acquisition of Collinson Hall, one of St Albans’ most historic estate agencies. Founded in 1825, Collinson Hall has been a staple in the local property market, offering residential sales, lettings, and block management services. The move sees the agency join Castles’ growing network of branches across Hertfordshire and Bedfordshire.

A Legacy Spanning Centuries

Collinson Hall’s history dates back nearly two centuries, making it one of the oldest estate agencies in the region. The firm was originally established as Collinsons before merging with Halls in the late 1990s. Directors Steven and Catherine Walker have been at the helm since the 1980s, guiding the business through several decades of change in the property market. Their leadership has cemented Collinson Hall’s reputation as a trusted name in St Albans.

The acquisition by Castles Property Group marks a new chapter for the agency, ensuring its continued presence in the local community. Castles, which operates nine branches across the region, has emphasised its commitment to maintaining Collinson Hall’s legacy while integrating it into its wider network.

Strengthening Regional Presence

For Castles Property Group, this acquisition represents a strategic move to enhance its footprint in Hertfordshire and Bedfordshire. The group has steadily expanded its operations in recent years, focusing on providing high-quality property services to homeowners and landlords. By bringing Collinson Hall into its portfolio, Castles aims to strengthen its position in the competitive local market.

Homeowners in the area may find reassurance in the continuity of service promised by both parties. Collinson Hall’s existing team will remain in place, ensuring that clients continue to receive the same level of expertise and personalised attention they have come to expect. Borrowers and property seekers might also benefit from the broader resources and support offered by Castles Property Group.

This acquisition highlights the ongoing consolidation within the estate agency sector, as larger groups look to absorb well-established local businesses. For Collinson Hall, the partnership with Castles offers an opportunity to leverage additional resources whilst maintaining its longstanding connection to the St Albans community.

As the property market continues to evolve, this deal underscores the importance of adaptability for estate agencies. Both Castles Property Group and Collinson Hall appear poised to navigate the challenges and opportunities ahead, ensuring their clients receive a seamless experience in their property journeys.

item.title: The property event that won’t let agents sleep
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Property professionals across the UK are gearing up for a unique 24-hour live broadcast event that combines industry insights with charitable fundraising. The 24-Hour Open House, organised by property consultant Toby Martin, will run continuously from 9am on 3rd September to 9am on 4th September, supporting Breast Cancer Now and Andy’s Man Club.

A marathon of property insights and entertainment

This innovative event will feature 48 half-hour sessions blending professional development with lighthearted content. The packed schedule includes training workshops, live podcast recordings, panel discussions and Q&A sessions with prominent industry figures. Confirmed speakers include behavioural science expert Phill Agnew, motivational speaker Nigel Risner, and Andy Barton, founder of Andy’s Man Club.

To keep energy levels high during the overnight hours, organisers have planned interactive entertainment including quiz segments and even a bedtime story session with children’s author Libby Elliott. Other property professionals taking part include Kirsty Franks, Alex Tan and Simon Whale, with more speakers expected to be announced.

Supporting vital charitable causes

While offering valuable networking and learning opportunities, the event primarily aims to raise funds for two important charities. Breast Cancer Now funds critical research into breast cancer treatment, while Andy’s Man Club provides mental health support groups for men across the UK.

Event sponsor Kotini has ensured all donations go directly to the charities. Their CEO Kieran Witt commented: “This initiative shows the property industry at its best – combining generosity with a touch of madness for worthwhile causes.”

Martin explained his motivation: “The property sector loves to talk, so why not do it for 24 hours straight? Behind the training sessions and fun activities, we’re supporting two organisations doing life-changing work. If losing a night’s sleep helps raise funds, it’s a small price to pay.”

Property professionals interested in joining this unique event can register for free online, with organisers encouraging participants to donate what they can to support these vital charities.

item.title: Stamp duty split leaves first-time buyers facing a postcode lottery
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

First-time buyers in England face a growing disparity in stamp duty costs depending on where they choose to purchase their home, according to recent analysis by Zoopla. The research highlights how regional property price differences have created a postcode lottery, with buyers in the South shouldering significantly higher tax burdens compared to those in the North. Whilst stamp duty relief for first-time buyers applies to properties up to £300,000, the likelihood of paying the tax varies dramatically across the country.

Regional disparities in stamp duty

Zoopla’s findings reveal that only 38% of first-time buyers nationally are required to pay stamp duty, meaning the majority complete their purchase without this additional cost. However, the picture changes considerably when broken down by region. In the North East, just 2.1% of first-time buyers face stamp duty, rising slightly to 3.8% in Yorkshire and the Humber, 6.2% in the North West, and 9.3% in the West Midlands. In these areas, average property prices remain comfortably below the £300,000 threshold, making stamp duty a rare concern.

By contrast, buyers in the South East and East of England face a much higher likelihood of paying stamp duty, with 51% and 52% of first-time buyers respectively affected. In these regions, the average purchase price for homes attracting stamp duty is close to £400,000. London presents the starkest challenge, with nearly 80% of first-time buyers liable for stamp duty. The average purchase price in the capital stands at £475,000, resulting in a median tax bill of £8,750—a significant upfront cost on top of the deposit.

Impact on homebuyers

The analysis underscores how regional house price disparities increasingly determine whether first-time buyers face an additional financial hurdle. For those in the North, the stamp duty relief threshold often aligns with local property prices, reducing the tax burden. However, in the South and particularly in London, where prices far exceed the £300,000 limit, stamp duty becomes a substantial consideration.

Homeowners planning to move also face varying costs depending on location. In the North East, for example, the median stamp duty bill for home movers is £1,500, compared to £20,000 in London. This growing divide highlights the challenges faced by buyers in high-demand areas, where higher property values translate into steeper tax liabilities.

Prospective buyers may wish to factor in stamp duty costs when budgeting for their purchase, particularly in regions where prices exceed the relief threshold. Whilst first-time buyers benefit from significant savings in many parts of the country, those in the South and London may need to account for this additional expense alongside their deposit and other upfront costs.

The findings from Zoopla emphasise the importance of understanding local market conditions and tax implications when planning a property purchase. With regional disparities continuing to shape the affordability landscape, buyers are encouraged to research thoroughly to ensure they are financially prepared for the costs involved.

item.title: Most tenants unaware of biggest rental shake-up in decades
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

New research reveals that nearly 70% of private renters in England remain unaware of the Renters’ Reform Bill, despite it representing the most substantial changes to rental laws in decades. The study, conducted by the TDS Charitable Foundation, highlights a worrying knowledge gap amongst tenants regarding their enhanced legal protections.

Key Reforms Going Unnoticed

The survey of over 2,000 private renters found that 69% either hadn’t heard of the legislation or didn’t understand its implications. This suggests many may miss out on crucial protections including:

  • The end of Section 21 ‘no-fault’ evictions
  • New restrictions on rent increases
  • Measures to prevent rental bidding wars
  • The right to request pet ownership

Particularly concerning is the lack of awareness among vulnerable groups, with 47% of students and many low-income renters completely unaware of the reforms. The research also found 78% of respondents didn’t know about tribunals for challenging excessive rent hikes.

Implementation Challenges Ahead

Experts warn that the legislation’s impact may be limited without better public understanding. Dr Jennifer Harris of TDS Charitable Foundation noted: “These rights only help tenants if they know they exist and how to use them. The findings show urgent work is needed to improve awareness, especially among those most likely to face rental difficulties.”

Interestingly, only 14% of renters said they’d use the new right to request pets, suggesting some headline measures may see limited uptake. The government now faces the challenge of ensuring all stakeholders – from landlords to advice services – play their part in educating tenants about these historic changes.

With the first provisions now in force, industry observers suggest renters may wish to familiarise themselves with the new protections that could significantly affect their tenancy rights and rental experiences moving forward.

item.title: Inherited property could face £120,000 capital gains tax hit under proposed reforms
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Families and property investors could face significantly higher Capital Gains Tax (CGT) bills under potential reforms being considered by policymakers, according to recent analysis. The proposed changes, which include abolishing the CGT uplift on death and aligning CGT rates with income tax, could have substantial financial implications for those inheriting or selling assets.

Potential Impact of CGT Reforms

Currently, when assets are inherited, they are rebased for CGT purposes, effectively wiping out any gains accrued during the deceased’s lifetime. However, if this relief were removed, beneficiaries could face substantial tax liabilities when selling inherited assets. Analysis suggests that a family inheriting a property that has increased in value by £500,000 could face a CGT bill of nearly £120,000 upon its sale.

Additionally, aligning CGT rates with income tax rates could further increase liabilities for investors. For example, an additional-rate taxpayer making a £50,000 gain could pay almost £10,000 more in tax, while a higher-rate taxpayer could see their bill rise by more than £7,500. Such changes could significantly alter the financial landscape for property investors and families managing inherited assets.

Broader Implications for Investors

The proposed reforms come at a time when policymakers are exploring ways to boost public finances. However, experts warn that increasing CGT burdens could discourage investment, potentially stifling economic growth. Investors often adjust their behaviour in response to tax changes, which could reduce the expected revenue gains from higher rates.

Ed Wood, financial planning director at Rathbones, noted: “There is a risk that further increases in the CGT burden could discourage investment at a time when the UK needs private capital to turbocharge economic growth.” This highlights the delicate balance between raising revenue and maintaining an environment conducive to investment.

Upcoming Inheritance Tax Changes

The potential CGT reforms coincide with planned changes to inheritance tax (IHT), which will bring unused pension funds within the scope of IHT from April 2027. Together, these measures could significantly impact how families plan for the future and manage inherited wealth.

For many, the removal of the CGT uplift on death could mean navigating complex financial decisions, particularly when selling inherited properties or other assets. Seeking professional financial advice may be essential to understanding the implications and planning effectively.

As discussions around these reforms continue, families and investors may wish to stay informed about potential changes and consider how they could affect their financial plans. With both CGT and IHT under scrutiny, proactive planning could help mitigate the impact of any future tax increases.

item.title: High-profile party leader to headline rent controls paper launch
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

The debate around rent controls in the UK’s private rental sector continues to gather momentum, with Green Party leader Zack Polanski set to headline a major policy paper launch in London tomorrow. The event will showcase new research examining the potential effects of rent regulation on affordability, public finances and landlord returns.

Key voices to discuss rent regulation

The policy paper, titled Taking Back Control of Rents: The Impact of Rent Regulation on Affordability, Public Spending and Landlord Profits, represents a collaboration between University College London’s Institute for Innovation and Public Purpose and the New Economics Foundation. Alongside Polanski, speakers will include housing policy expert Beth Stratford, economist Josh Ryan-Collins and researcher Pete Ely.

This comes as the Green Party continues to push rent controls as a central policy, with Polanski having previously described the current rental market as part of “rip-off Britain”. In earlier comments, he suggested that freezing rents from autumn 2022 could have saved tenants an average of £3,300 annually, potentially injecting £18bn back into local economies.

Growing calls for rental market reform

The launch event follows increasing political discussion about intervention in the private rental sector. The Greens have positioned themselves as advocates for tenants’ rights, criticising what they view as excessive landlord profits at the expense of community vitality. Polanski has previously argued that current rental costs are “hollowing out” high streets by reducing disposable income.

While the Labour government recently introduced its Renters Rights Bill without including rent controls, the Green Party maintains that such measures could help rebalance the housing market. Their proposals include linking rent increases to local wages or inflation, rather than market rates.

For homeowners and landlords monitoring these developments, the debate highlights ongoing tensions between housing affordability and property investment returns. With housing costs remaining a key concern for many households, policymakers continue to grapple with finding solutions that satisfy both tenants and property owners.

As the discussion evolves, events like tomorrow’s launch may help shape future policy directions in the UK’s increasingly complex rental market.

item.title: Estate agents grapple with £21m rise in property fall-through costs
item.author: Rizky Aditya Pratama
item.description:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

The UK property market saw a notable rise in collapsed transactions during the first quarter of 2026, with new data revealing a £21m increase in associated costs compared to the previous quarter. According to analysis by the House Buyer Bureau, 67,489 residential sales fell through between January and March – a 9.8% quarterly increase, though still 12.1% lower than the same period in 2025.

Growing financial impact on the housing market

The surge in failed transactions pushed the estimated total cost to the housing sector from £218.3m in Q4 2025 to £239.2m in early 2026. While the average cost per collapsed sale dipped slightly by 0.2% to £3,544, the higher volume of fall-throughs created a greater cumulative financial burden. This figure remains 2.1% higher than a year earlier, reflecting ongoing pressures from inflation, rising legal fees and shifting property values.

For estate agents, the increase translates to more time invested in sales that ultimately fail to complete. Homeowners typically bear the brunt of these setbacks, often needing to relist properties and absorb additional marketing costs. The data suggests that whilst individual losses haven’t changed dramatically, the sheer number of disrupted transactions is creating significant ripple effects across the market.

Understanding the causes behind transaction failures

Industry experts highlight multiple factors contributing to the rise in collapsed sales. Chris Hodgkinson of House Buyer Bureau notes that affordability challenges, shifting buyer circumstances and broader economic uncertainty continue to jeopardise transactions at late stages. Mortgage affordability checks, survey issues and chain breaks also frequently play a role.

Prospective buyers might consider these strategies to improve transaction security:

  • Obtaining a mortgage agreement in principle before making offers
  • Commissioning thorough property surveys early in the process
  • Maintaining open communication with all parties in the chain
  • Considering fall-through protection insurance where available

Whilst some factors remain beyond participants’ control, preparation and professional guidance could help reduce the risk of costly delays. As the market continues to adjust to economic conditions, both buyers and sellers may benefit from factoring potential setbacks into their timelines and budgets.
item.content:encoded:
By Rizky Aditya Pratama

The UK property market saw a notable rise in collapsed transactions during the first quarter of 2026, with new data revealing a £21m increase in associated costs compared to the previous quarter. According to analysis by the House Buyer Bureau, 67,489 residential sales fell through between January and March – a 9.8% quarterly increase, though still 12.1% lower than the same period in 2025.

Growing financial impact on the housing market

The surge in failed transactions pushed the estimated total cost to the housing sector from £218.3m in Q4 2025 to £239.2m in early 2026. While the average cost per collapsed sale dipped slightly by 0.2% to £3,544, the higher volume of fall-throughs created a greater cumulative financial burden. This figure remains 2.1% higher than a year earlier, reflecting ongoing pressures from inflation, rising legal fees and shifting property values.

For estate agents, the increase translates to more time invested in sales that ultimately fail to complete. Homeowners typically bear the brunt of these setbacks, often needing to relist properties and absorb additional marketing costs. The data suggests that whilst individual losses haven’t changed dramatically, the sheer number of disrupted transactions is creating significant ripple effects across the market.

Understanding the causes behind transaction failures

Industry experts highlight multiple factors contributing to the rise in collapsed sales. Chris Hodgkinson of House Buyer Bureau notes that affordability challenges, shifting buyer circumstances and broader economic uncertainty continue to jeopardise transactions at late stages. Mortgage affordability checks, survey issues and chain breaks also frequently play a role.

Prospective buyers might consider these strategies to improve transaction security:

  • Obtaining a mortgage agreement in principle before making offers
  • Commissioning thorough property surveys early in the process
  • Maintaining open communication with all parties in the chain
  • Considering fall-through protection insurance where available

Whilst some factors remain beyond participants’ control, preparation and professional guidance could help reduce the risk of costly delays. As the market continues to adjust to economic conditions, both buyers and sellers may benefit from factoring potential setbacks into their timelines and budgets.

item.title: UK house prices and market activity ‘holding up better than we dared hope’
item.author: Sri Wahyuni Astuti
item.description:
By Sri Wahyuni Astuti

The UK housing market showed tentative signs of stabilisation in June, with some indicators performing better than expected despite ongoing economic pressures. According to the latest Royal Institution of Chartered Surveyors (RICS) report, while activity remains subdued, the pace of decline has slowed in several key areas, offering cautious optimism for the months ahead.

Market Activity Shows Gradual Improvement

New buyer enquiries remained in negative territory with a net balance of -29%, though this represented an improvement from -34% in previous months and marked the least negative reading since February. Agreed sales also saw a marginal uptick, moving from -35% in May to -32% in June. Whilst these figures still indicate challenging conditions, the gradual improvement suggests some resilience in the market.

Survey respondents pointed to easing geopolitical tensions and slightly lower mortgage rates as potential factors supporting confidence. Expectations for sales activity over the next three months improved significantly, with the net balance rising to -16% from March’s low of -34%. Looking further ahead, professionals anticipate sales volumes to remain broadly stable over the next year, with a modest net balance of +1%.

Supply Constraints and Regional Variations

The supply of available properties continued to tighten in June, with new vendor instructions falling to a net balance of -23% – the weakest reading in over a year. Market appraisals also declined, suggesting that the shortage of homes for sale could persist in the coming months.

House prices remained under pressure nationally, with a net balance of -33% reporting declines. Regional variations were notable, with the South East and South West experiencing the weakest price trends, whilst Scotland and Northern Ireland maintained more positive conditions. Near-term price expectations improved slightly, rising to -32% from -44%, and over the next 12 months, respondents were cautiously optimistic, with a net balance of +8% anticipating price increases.

Tom Bill, head of UK residential research at Knight Frank, noted: "The market is recovering from a low base as mortgage rates edge lower and some global uncertainties ease. However, speculation around potential property tax changes in the upcoming Budget may continue to weigh on buyer and seller confidence."

With supply constraints and gradual improvements in demand, the UK housing market appears to be navigating a delicate balance. Homeowners and prospective buyers may wish to monitor these trends closely as the summer progresses.

item.title: Rightmove takes its AI strategy a step further
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

Rightmove has enhanced its property search experience with an expanded artificial intelligence feature that lets prospective buyers and renters ask natural language questions about individual listings. The 'Ask Rightmove' tool, initially launched on the portal's homepage earlier this year, now appears across property pages in a phased rollout.

How the AI property assistant works

The conversational tool allows users to ask practical questions about homes and their surroundings, with responses generated from multiple data sources. Home-hunters might inquire about local amenities, transport links or property specifics like parking availability. The system combines:

  • Listing details provided by estate agents
  • Local area information from geospatial data
  • Market insights from Rightmove's property database

Developed in partnership with Google Cloud using Gemini AI models, the feature aims to help users make more informed decisions before contacting agents. Early data suggests visitors using the tool demonstrate higher purchase intent.

Part of Rightmove's broader AI strategy

This rollout forms part of Rightmove's wider investment in artificial intelligence across its platform. The portal has introduced several AI-powered tools including:

  • Style with AI for property descriptions
  • Opportunity Manager to help agents identify potential sellers
  • Rightmove Discover for lead generation

Tarah Lourens, Rightmove's Chief Technology and Operations Officer, commented: "Understanding whether a property meets specific needs and learning about the local area are crucial steps in the home-moving process. Our technology allows people to get this information in their own words."

As property portals continue to integrate advanced technologies, these developments may change how buyers and renters research homes. While AI tools can provide helpful preliminary information, prospective purchasers will still need to verify details and seek professional advice before making property decisions.

item.title: First house price rise in four months
item.author: Bambang Setiawan
item.description:
By Bambang Setiawan

UK house prices have shown their first monthly increase since March 2026, offering a glimmer of positivity in what has been a subdued property market. However, industry analysts suggest this uptick may not signal the start of a sustained recovery, with only modest growth predicted for the remainder of the year.

A tentative recovery in housing market

The latest figures reveal a 0.3% rise in average property values across the UK during July, marking the first monthly gain after four consecutive months of decline or stagnation. This marginal improvement comes amidst relatively stable mortgage rates and improved buyer confidence following the spring slowdown.

Regional variations continue to play a significant role, with some areas experiencing stronger rebounds than others. The South East and London have shown particular resilience, while more affordable northern regions maintain steady demand from first-time buyers.

Outlook for the rest of 2026

Property experts remain cautiously optimistic about the market's trajectory, though few anticipate dramatic price surges. Several factors are expected to influence the market in coming months:

  • Mortgage rates stabilising after previous fluctuations
  • Ongoing affordability pressures for many buyers
  • Potential impact of autumn government policies
  • Seasonal patterns typically showing stronger activity post-summer

Economists suggest that annual price growth could settle between 1-2% by year-end, representing a more sustainable pace compared to the volatile swings seen earlier in the decade. Homeowners considering selling may find autumn presents reasonable market conditions, while buyers might benefit from less frenetic competition than during peak spring periods.

As the property market enters its traditionally busier autumn season, prospective buyers and sellers would be wise to monitor local market conditions closely and seek professional mortgage advice tailored to their individual circumstances.

item.title: Average size of landlord portfolios grows
item.author: Nadya Putri Maharani
item.description:
By Nadya Putri Maharani

Recent industry analysis suggests the average size of UK landlord property portfolios has increased, with more investors adopting professional business approaches. This trend reflects a growing shift towards limited company ownership structures amongst buy-to-let investors.

Professionalisation of the buy-to-let sector

The private rental sector has seen significant changes in recent years, with many landlords expanding their portfolios despite tax and regulatory challenges. Industry observers note that experienced investors are increasingly treating property as a formal business rather than a casual investment.

Several factors appear to be driving this professionalisation:

  • The growing complexity of tax rules for private landlords
  • Changing mortgage products designed for portfolio landlords
  • Increased competition in the rental market
  • The potential benefits of limited company structures

Limited company ownership on the rise

Property investors might consider that operating through a limited company could offer certain advantages, particularly for those with multiple properties. While this approach isn't suitable for everyone, it has become increasingly popular amongst portfolio landlords.

Key considerations for limited company buy-to-let include:

  • Different mortgage interest tax treatment compared to personal ownership
  • Potential inheritance tax planning benefits
  • Separate legal identity from personal finances
  • Additional accounting and administrative requirements

Those exploring this route may wish to seek professional tax advice, as individual circumstances can significantly affect the suitability of different ownership structures.

As the buy-to-let market continues to evolve, landlords appear to be adapting their strategies to maintain profitability in a changing financial landscape. The growth in portfolio sizes suggests that despite recent challenges, property investment remains an attractive option for many.
